---
title: "Moving Averages"
description: "SMA, EMA, and WMA — the building blocks of trend-following strategies."
date: 2026-04-01
categories: [signal-processing, trend]
---

## Simple Moving Average (SMA)

$$
\text{SMA}_t(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} x_{t-i}
$$

```{python}
import numpy as np

def sma(x: np.ndarray, n: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """Simple moving average with a window of n periods."""
    kernel = np.ones(n) / n
    return np.convolve(x, kernel, mode="valid")
```

## Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The EMA gives more weight to recent observations via a smoothing factor $\alpha \in (0,1)$:

$$
\text{EMA}_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{EMA}_{t-1}
$$

A common choice is $\alpha = \dfrac{2}{n+1}$ for an $n$-period EMA.

```{python}
def ema(x: np.ndarray, n: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """Exponential moving average."""
    alpha = 2 / (n + 1)
    out = np.empty_like(x, dtype=float)
    out[0] = x[0]
    for t in range(1, len(x)):
        out[t] = alpha * x[t] + (1 - alpha) * out[t - 1]
    return out
```

::: {.callout-tip}
The EMA reacts faster to price changes than the SMA because recent data carries
more weight. This makes it more useful for short-term signal detection.
:::

## Comparison

| Property | SMA | EMA |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Lag | Higher | Lower |
| Noise sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Computational cost | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per step |
| Memory needed | Window of $n$ values | Single state value |
